title: Bobby And The Critic

jokes.text: I didn't know ham could keep that long under hot lights.
jokes.setup: Bobby talks about performing alone on stage for 90 minutes
jokes.punchline: Louie compares Bobby to ham that spoils under theater lights
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Louie - cruel but clever wordplay that reframes Bobby's acting aspirations through food preservation metaphors. Well-crafted insult.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: 90 minutes? That's a long time to watch something go bad under the lights. You'll be like ham in a window.

jokes.text: Have you ever met, or do you know, anybody who's ever won one of those contests?
jokes.setup: Someone mentions contest coupons from food purchase
jokes.punchline: The realistic observation that nobody ever wins those contests
jokes.explanation: Standard observational humor about contest scams. Relatable but not particularly clever or character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Nobody ever wins those contests. You know why? Because they don't want anybody to win. It's a scam.

dialogue.speaker: Alex Reiger
dialogue.line: Come on, Louie, somebody has to win.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Yeah, sure. Their cousin. Their nephew. Some guy in Des Moines who doesn't even exist.

jokes.text: I do. Who? Me. I have won. I have a match. I won a complete men's wardrobe.
jokes.setup: The skeptical question about anyone winning contests
jokes.punchline: Latka immediately contradicts the assumption by claiming victory
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that subverts the setup. Latka's earnest delivery makes it work, though the concept is straightforward.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I won a contest!

jokes.text: No, no. A fly swatter.
jokes.setup: Expectation that Latka won money in a lottery
jokes.punchline: He won a fly swatter instead
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect economy. The absurdity of winning a fly swatter in a lottery is both unexpected and character-appropriate for Latka.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I won the lottery! I'm a rich man!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You won the lottery? Let me see that ticket.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Here it is.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: This says you won a fly swatter.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yes! A fly swatter! I'm rich!

jokes.text: No, he was a good worker and a very nice man.
jokes.setup: Someone calls the fly swatter prize insufficient
jokes.punchline: Latka treats the fly swatter as a person with personal qualities
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor that perfectly captures Latka's unique worldview. The personification of a fly swatter is unexpected and memorably strange.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: What do you mean, insufficient? This is a beautiful fly swatter. It has a nice handle, good balance. You could take it anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: It's a very sensitive fly swatter. You have to treat it with respect.

jokes.text: who got her cat's head stuck in her mouth?
jokes.setup: Jim asks if anyone read a news story about a woman
jokes.punchline: The impossible physical scenario of a cat's head in a woman's mouth
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment - the absurd reversal of expected physics fits his character's unique logic. Memorable for its impossible imagery.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Did anyone read that story about the woman who had a cat's head in her mouth?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: How is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I don't know, but apparently she swallowed.

jokes.text: [Awkward silence and topic change after Jim's bizarre story]
jokes.setup: Jim's completely nonsensical news story
jokes.punchline: The uncomfortable silence as everyone processes this impossibility
jokes.explanation: Standard reaction beat to absurdist humor. The silence itself becomes the joke as characters can't respond to Jim's impossible scenario.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know, I was reading about this study where they found that if you eat nothing but cabbage for a month, you can actually fly.

jokes.text: A 30-millimeter cannon fired at the stage during production could not hit anything worth saving.
jokes.setup: Bobby reading John Bowman's harsh theater review
jokes.punchline: The critic suggests military weapons couldn't improve the show
jokes.explanation: Brilliant professional comedy writing - the military metaphor for theatrical destruction is both specific and hyperbolic in a memorable way.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Listen to this: 'Even if this production were bombed by the Air Force, it couldn't be destroyed enough.'

jokes.text: Hey, I'd like to see that. What time do they shoot off the cannon?
jokes.setup: The metaphorical cannon review
jokes.punchline: Tony takes it literally and wants to see the actual cannon
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Tony's literal interpretation of the metaphor fits his straightforward personality. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: A cannon review? I want to see the cannon!

jokes.text: it should henceforth be a felony to yell 'Patty Ganzel' in a crowded theater.
jokes.setup: Reading about an actress's terrible performance
jokes.punchline: Parody of 'shouting fire in a crowded theater' with the actress's name
jokes.explanation: Exceptional wordplay that cleverly subverts a famous legal phrase. Professional-level comedy writing with perfect economy and memorable execution.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You know, there's a famous quote about free speech: you can't shout 'fire' in a crowded theater.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: But you CAN shout 'Jennifer Connelly' in a crowded theater. Everyone will just assume she's performing.

jokes.text: 'Slime wad' is two words.
jokes.setup: Bobby writing an angry letter to the critic
jokes.punchline: Alex's helpful proofreading of an insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Alex as the helpful straight man. The mundane editing help during an emotional rant creates good contrast comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Dear Sir, You are an idiot.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Bobby, you can't send that. It's too harsh.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: What do you suggest?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: How about: 'Dear Sir, While I respect your opinion, I feel you may have missed the point of my performance.'

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: That's too nice.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, you could say: 'Dear Sir, You are an idiot, but a well-intentioned one.'

jokes.text: Purple. Not as close.
jokes.setup: Bobby asking for strong adjectives for his angry letter
jokes.punchline: Elaine offers 'purple' which is acknowledged as unhelpful
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor based on offering an irrelevant adjective. Works as a small beat but not particularly memorable or clever.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I need a strong adjective for my angry letter. Something really powerful.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: How about 'purple'?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Purple? That's not even an adjective that expresses anger.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, it's a color.

jokes.text: Fate.
jokes.setup: Louie's internal struggle about whether to mail Bobby's letter
jokes.punchline: He justifies sending it with a single dramatic word
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character work. Louie's faux-philosophical justification for his mischief is perfectly delivered in one word.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I shouldn't send this letter. It's wrong. Bobby trusted me with this.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But what if she needs to know? What if this changes everything?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No. No, I can't. I won't betray that trust.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But... destiny.

jokes.text: Did the lady get the cat's head out of her mouth?
jokes.setup: Jim's earlier impossible news story
jokes.punchline: He continues to treat this impossible scenario as real news
jokes.explanation: Good callback that maintains Jim's consistent absurdist worldview. The persistence of the impossible scenario adds to its comedic value.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, I'm just reporting what happened. A man walked into a bar, ordered a drink, and turned into a chimpanzee. That's the story.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Look, I don't make the news, I just report it. The chimpanzee ordered another round, left a two-dollar tip, and walked out.

jokes.text: Well, I know a cat and a lady who care plenty.
jokes.setup: Someone dismisses Jim's concern about the cat story
jokes.punchline: Jim seriously considers the feelings of participants in an impossible scenario
jokes.explanation: Strong callback that deepens Jim's commitment to the absurd premise. His empathy for impossible creatures is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, what about the cat's feelings?

jokes.text: Louie, how could you?
jokes.setup: Discovery that Louie sent Bobby's letter without permission
jokes.punchline: Elaine's predictable moral outrage
jokes.explanation: Standard reaction with no particular craft or surprise. Functional for plot advancement but not comedically notable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You opened Bobby's letter? You sent it without asking him?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: He asked me to mail it.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He asked you to mail it, not to open it! That's a violation of his privacy!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I had to make sure the address was right.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That is such a typical excuse. You're just like every other authority figure who thinks they have the right to invade people's privacy in the name of 'helping' them.

jokes.text: This fortress... He's going to get in. No way. This is hermetically sealed, this cage.
jokes.setup: Bobby trying to get into Louie's dispatcher cage to attack him
jokes.punchline: Louie's overconfident description of his protective barrier
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Louie's cowardice disguised as bravado. The 'hermetically sealed' description is amusingly overblown.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You can't get in here, Wheeler. This cage is made of reinforced steel. Nothing can penetrate it.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Oh yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: *throws a punch through the cage*

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Okay, so maybe it's not reinforced.

jokes.text: Hey, Bobby, it's your agent. My agent's calling me?! Okay, okay, Louie, you stay right there.
jokes.setup: Bobby in the middle of confronting Louie
jokes.punchline: Immediately abandons his anger when his agent calls
jokes.explanation: Perfect actor character moment - career always comes first, even over righteous anger.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Louie, I've had it with you! I'm sick of the way you treat people around here. You're a terrible dispatcher and an even worse—

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Hello? Yes, this is he. Oh, hi Stan!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, of course I remember you. You got me that callback for the Tampax commercial. How are you?

jokes.text: Now I'm a hero and great-looking.
jokes.setup: Bobby receiving praise for his letter and feeling confident
jokes.punchline: He adds appearance to his list of newfound virtues
jokes.explanation: Mild character comedy showing Bobby's vanity and tendency to get carried away. The non-sequitur about looks is mildly amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You know, I'm starting to realize I've got a lot of good qualities. I'm intelligent, I'm sensitive, I'm compassionate.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: And now I can add one more thing to that list: I'm incredibly handsome.

jokes.text: Oh, God. Bing.
jokes.setup: Bobby realizes the critic he insulted is coming to review him
jokes.punchline: His defeated repetition of the charades clue
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment that captures Bobby's dawning horror. The repetition of 'Bing' shows his mental state deteriorating.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: A movie? A book? A play? A movie? A book? A play?

jokes.text: Hey, Bob, that was... A disaster. No, Bobby. The play stinks. I stink.
jokes.setup: Tony tries to encourage Bobby after the first act
jokes.punchline: Tony accidentally confirms Bobby's fears while trying to help
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Tony's well-meaning but tactless nature. The failed encouragement creates effective cringe humor.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Bobby, you gotta have confidence. You're a good driver. You got skills.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You really think so?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Of course! I mean, compared to some of the meatheads around here, you're actually... well, you're definitely better than the worst.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Better than the worst?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Look, you're not the greatest, but you're not terrible. You're... you're somewhere in the middle. Basically.

jokes.text: I don't know why everybody say it is too long and boring. You know, I enjoyed it and to me, I think it was just right. Well, there's a second act coming up, Latka. Not for me, there isn't.
jokes.setup: Latka appears to praise Bobby's performance
jokes.punchline: He's leaving before the second act, revealing his true opinion
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that uses Latka's innocent delivery to devastating effect. The timing of the revelation maximizes the comedic impact.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Bobby, you were wonderful in the first act!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Thank you, Latka! That means a lot coming from you.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, so wonderful that I must leave now before the second act begins.

jokes.text: Ha! You crazy guy. Charles Darwin, eh? Loony!
jokes.setup: Jim commenting on Bobby's Darwin performance
jokes.punchline: Jim dismisses Darwin himself as crazy rather than the performance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment - his unique perspective makes Darwin the target rather than Bobby's portrayal. Shows his distinctive worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know, I've seen worse performances of Darwin. In fact, I think Darwin himself was crazier than Bobby's version.

jokes.text: Yeah, I guess it feels that way, huh? No, Darwin is 40 years older in Act Two.
jokes.setup: Tony assumes Bobby feels like he's aging on stage
jokes.punchline: Bobby means it literally - the character ages in the play
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection joke about literal vs. metaphorical aging.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You feel like you're aging on stage?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: No, I mean literally. The character ages in the play. I start as a 25-year-old and by the third act I'm 67.

jokes.text: Uh, Jim, why don't you just keep it?
jokes.setup: Jim asks to borrow Bobby's comb
jokes.punchline: Bobby's disgusted reaction suggests Jim's hygiene makes the comb unsalvageable
jokes.explanation: Standard gross-out humor based on Jim's hygiene issues. Functional but not particularly clever or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Can I borrow your comb?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: My comb? Are you kidding? You'll get it all greasy and matted.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'll give it back in perfect condition.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: That's what I'm afraid of.

jokes.text: I mean, in that second act, you were sweating like a pig. I've never, never, never seen anybody sweat like that. That's what I call talent.
jokes.setup: Tony trying to compliment Bobby's performance
jokes.punchline: He focuses on Bobby's obvious nervousness as a positive attribute
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Tony's well-meaning but misguided attempt at encouragement. His focus on sweating as talent is amusingly wrongheaded.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know, Bobby, I gotta say, you were really nervous out there.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, thanks a lot, Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, no, I mean it as a compliment. That nervousness? That's authenticity. That's real emotion.

jokes.text: Mr. Carbuncle? My name's Jim Ignatowski.
jokes.setup: John Bowman mentions Bobby called him 'the carbuncle on the butt of the American theater'
jokes.punchline: Jim mistakes this as Bowman's actual name and introduces himself
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy that perfectly captures Jim's literal-minded confusion. The formal introduction to 'Mr. Carbuncle' is absurd and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Jim Ignatowski. Nice to meet you, Carbuncle.

dialogue.speaker: John Bowman
dialogue.line: It's Bowman. John Bowman.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, sorry. Nice to meet you, John Carbuncle.

jokes.text: Is he the playwright?
jokes.setup: Jim's confusion about who Bowman wants to speak with
jokes.punchline: Jim still doesn't understand he's not the target of the conversation
jokes.explanation: Continuation of Jim's confusion that extends the previous joke. Works as part of the sequence but less impactful on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, you want to talk to me?

dialogue.speaker: Bowman
dialogue.line: No, I want to talk to Bobby.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: But I'm Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Bowman
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: Are you insane? That play is an excellent argument for dying young.
jokes.setup: Bobby asks if Bowman liked the play
jokes.punchline: Bowman's brutal assessment that the play makes death seem preferable
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor that establishes Bowman's character perfectly. The 'dying young' metaphor is both shocking and precisely crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Did you like the play?

dialogue.speaker: John Bowman
dialogue.line: Like it? Bobby, that play made death seem preferable.

jokes.text: you can keep on acting in church basements and supermarket openings, and your own mirror
jokes.setup: Bowman explaining what anonymity means for Bobby's career
jokes.punchline: The progression from bad venues to ultimately performing for oneself
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that builds to the perfect endpoint of performing for mirrors. The progression is both logical and devastating.

dialogue.speaker: John Bowman
dialogue.line: You see Bobby, anonymity means you start in the worst clubs, then you move up to slightly better clubs, then you get to play the good rooms, then you play the really great venues, and eventually...

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Eventually what?

dialogue.speaker: John Bowman
dialogue.line: Eventually you're performing for yourself in your apartment.

jokes.text: No review will keep you anonymous and you can keep on acting in church basements and supermarket openings, and your own mirror for the rest of your life.
jokes.setup: Bowman explaining his power over Bobby's career
jokes.punchline: Escalating list ending with acting to his own reflection
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with the mirror detail being both pathetic and specific. Great villain moment.

dialogue.speaker: Bowman
dialogue.line: I control Bobby's career. I could tell him to stop acting, and he'd have to listen to me.

dialogue.speaker: Bowman
dialogue.line: I could tell him to act only in dinner theater productions.

dialogue.speaker: Bowman
dialogue.line: I could tell him to act only in his apartment.

dialogue.speaker: Bowman
dialogue.line: I could tell him to act only to his own reflection in the mirror.

jokes.text: Thank you.
jokes.setup: Bobby calls Bowman's behavior the cruelest thing he's ever seen
jokes.punchline: Bowman takes this as a compliment
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment with maximum economy. Bowman's simple 'thank you' reveals his complete moral inversion in just two words.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: That's the cruelest thing I've ever seen.

dialogue.speaker: John Bowman
dialogue.line: Why, thank you, Bobby.

jokes.text: Right, and you thought you stunk!
jokes.setup: Bobby declares that only his opinion of his performance matters
jokes.punchline: Tony immediately reminds him of his negative self-assessment
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflation of Bobby's moment of self-empowerment. Tony's timing perfectly undercuts the inspirational moment with reality.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Only my opinion of my performance matters.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You're right. And didn't you say you were terrible?

tmdb_overview: Bobby, who is appearing in a one man play, is fired up about a ruthless theater critic who has been writing scathing reviews.  Bobby writes an angry letter to the critic, but throws it away without sending it, having already felt better for venting his anger.  Predictably, Louie fishes it out of the trash and mails it, and it gets printed in the newspaper.  Bobby is soon getting cheers from actors all over the city, but gets worried when he learns that the critic himself is going to be at Bobby's play.  After the play, the critic finds Bobby and hands him a wonderful review of his performance.  Bobby is overcome until the critic says he's not going to print it, because rather than a scathing review which would seem like sour grapes or a good review that would boost his career, not submitting a review will leave Bobby in obscurity and the critic will have his vengeance.
insight: Misdirection-heavy episode delivers 33 jokes in tight 18-minute package despite middling impact scores.
